data.title: Abacus downgraded at Piper Sandler after Q4 results on 'ambitious' long-term targets
data.description:
Abacus Global Management (ABX) was trading lower as Piper Sandler cut its recommendation on the stock after Q4 results, citing long-term targets could prove ambitious.

Shares were 4.06% lower to $9.45 on Friday morning trading.

The alternative asset manager reported Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $0.23, which beats by $0.04, and revenue of $71.9M (+116.6% Y/Y), which beats by $16.84M.

As part of its full-year 2026 outlook, Abacus said adjusted net income is expected to be between $96M and $104M (growth of up to 22% year-over-year).

"ABX reported a core beat with strength from its life solutions segment. In addition, the company issued 2026 adj net income guidance of $100M at the mid-point, in-line with the Street, but below PSC," said analysts Crispin Love and Ben Graham.

"Beyond 2026, ABX is targeting adj EBITDA growing to $450M in 2030 versus $133M in 2025, implying a 28% CAGR. The company also expects AUM to increase to $50B in 2030 from $4B in 2025, which we view as aggressive," said the research note.

"We are decreasing our 2026 and 2027 core EPS estimates to $0.96 and $1.11 from $1.05 and $1.15," said the analysts.

Piper Sandler downgraded the stock to Neutral from Overweight, setting the price target at $11.00.

The investment bank's rating aligns with Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating of Hold. Meanwhile, the Wall Street analysts and SA authors grade the stock as Strong Buy.

data.title: Nektar Therapeutics jumps on Q4 results
data.description:
Nektar Therapeutics (NKTR) is up 6% during early-trading hours on Friday after the biopharmaceutical company beat fourth-quarter revenue and EPS estimates.

For the fourth quarter, the company reported GAAP EPS of -$1.78, beating estimates by $0.69, while revenue of $21.81M was $11.4M higher than estimates.

However, revenue for the full year of 2025 was $55.2 million, compared with $98.4 million in 2024.

The company stated that revenue primarily decreased year-over-year because it no longer recognizes product sales due to the December 2024 sale of the Huntsville manufacturing facility, along with a decrease in non-cash royalty revenue.  

"2025 was a pivotal year for Nektar as we saw successful and transformative Phase 2 data readouts for rezpegaldesleukin," said Howard W. Robin, President and CEO of Nektar. "The data highlighted the promise and differentiation of our novel Treg mechanism in two inflammatory dermatological disease settings of atopic dermatitis and alopecia areata. In early 2026, we reported the 52-week treatment data for rezpegaldesleukin. These data provide hope that complete clearance of disease could be possible for patients with monthly and quarterly maintenance dosing of rezpegaldesleukin. With our strengthened financial position following the recent financing, we look forward to initiating our Phase 3 program in atopic dermatitis in June of this year, while we continue to advance our earlier TNFR2 agonist antibody and bispecific program toward the clinic."

The company’s CFO, Sandra Gardiner, provided preliminary 2026 guidance of non-cash royalty revenue of $40 million to $45 million and R&D expenses between $200 million and $250 million. Gardiner added that the firm expects to end 2026 with $400 million to $460 million in cash and investments.

data.title: France and Italy open talks with Iran seeking safe passage through Strait of Hormuz - FT
data.description:
Some European governments have opened ‌talks with Iran trying ​to ​negotiate a deal to ⁠guarantee ​safe passage ​for their ships through the ​Strait of ​Hormuz, the Financial ‌Times ⁠reported Friday.

France and Italy reportedly have opened the tentative discussions in an attempt to restart oil and gas exports without expanding the conflict, as shipments through Hormuz - the narrow waterway through which ~20% of the world’s oil and liquefied natural gas normally flows - have slowed to a trickle following Iranian attacks on tankers and a pledge from the country's new supreme leader to keep it closed.

It is not known if the talks could produce an agreements or if Iran is willing to negotiate, but European governments are concerned that any sustained blockage of the strait could raise energy costs for businesses and households, adding to the continent's economic woes and strained national budgets.

Italy, France and Greece have warships in the Red Sea as part of a European Union naval protection mission, but no European navies are expected to escort ships through Hormuz if there is a risk of attack, for fear of escalating the war, FT said.

Crude oil prices have climbed to as high as $100/bbl from ~$60/bbl at the beginning of the year, while European natural gas prices have surged 75% during the same period.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for April delivery recently -1.3% to $94.43/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for May delivery was -0.6% to $99.88/bbl.

ETFs: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (XLE)

data.title: Citigroup and Goldman Sachs top growth factor grades among S&P financial holdings
data.description:
The S&P 500 has experienced a challenging two-week period marked by escalating geopolitical tensions, sector rotation into defensives, and technical deterioration.

In light of this, below is a list of the top 10 S&P financial holdings arranged according to their growth factor grade, a quantitative measure used to evaluate a company’s expansion trajectory.

The list is topped by Citigroup and The Goldman Sachs Group (GS), both earning an A growth grade. Wells Fargo & Company (WFC) and Bank of America (BAC) follow with B grades. The remaining holdings range from B- down to D, with JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) receiving a D+ and Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A) earning a D grade, placing them in sell territory.

The growth factor grade is a quantitative assessment used in stock analysis to evaluate a company’s growth prospects and expansion trajectory. This metric systematically analyzes multiple growth-related indicators to determine how effectively a company is scaling its business operations.

Growth factor grades are typically expressed on a letter scale (A+ through F), where higher grades indicate stronger growth characteristics relative to sector peers. An A+ grade represents exceptional growth potential, while lower grades suggest more moderate expansion or potential headwinds.

Here is the list:

  1. Citigroup, Growth grade: A

  2. Goldman Sachs (GS), Growth grade: A

  3. Wells Fargo (WFC), Growth grade: B

  4. Bank of America (BAC), Growth grade: B

  5. American Express (AXP), Growth grade: B-

  6. Mastercard (MA), Growth grade: B-

  7. Visa (V), Growth grade: B-

  8. Morgan Stanley (MS), Growth grade: C+

  9. JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Growth grade: D+

  10. Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A), Growth grade: D

data.title: Meta strikes news content deal with WSJ owner, leading European media firms
data.description:
Meta (META) will start offering more real-time news content on its platforms, which will be sourced from News Corp (NWS) (NWSA), France's Le Figaro, Spain's Prisa, and Germany's Süddeutsche Zeitung.

Financial terms of the deal were not publicly disclosed, but a Wall Street Journal report from earlier this month said Meta's deal with News Corp was struck at $50M per year annually.

"We’ll continue to add new partnerships and explore new features to enhance the experience for the people who use our products," Meta said Friday.

Meta and its rivals Google (GOOG) (GOOGL), Amazon (AMZN), and OpenAI (OPENAI) have similar agreements in place with other leading news outlets, as content from trusted and reputable sources is used extensively to train large AI models.

The New York Times (NYT) has a three-year deal with Google for $100M and up to $25M per year with Amazon, while the ChatGPT owner has a $250M deal with News Corp spanning over five years.

data.title: Wall Street advances as investors assess PCE inflation and GDP data
data.description:
Wall Street’s major indexes moved higher on Friday as investors digested a fresh batch of economic data, highlighted by the latest PCE inflation and GDP reports.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was +0.4%, the blue-chip Dow (DJI) was +0.4% and the tech focused Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.4%. 

From a sector-by-sector stance, 10 of the 11 S&P sectors were in the green with utilities being the strongest performing area on the session while materials has been the weakest. 

On the economic front, core PCE inflation rose 3.1% Y/Y in January, a touch hotter than expected. Furthermore, the U.S. GDP growth estimate was revised lower to +0.7% in Q4. 

Additionally, durable goods orders growth has stalled in the month of January. The January job openings increased more than expected in the latest JOLTS report and consumer sentiment data declined more than expected in March

U.S. Treasury yields ticked lower across the curve. The 2-year Treasury yield (US2Y) stood at 3.67%, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) dipped to 4.24%, and the 30-year Treasury yield (US30Y) was at 4.87%. 

As for stocks that are on the move, Darden Restaurants (DRI) climbed up +2.5%, and shares of Adobe (ADBE) fell -6.4%.

data.title: Consumer sentiment declines more than expected in March
data.description:
Consumer Sentiment Index: 55.5 in March vs. 56.2 consensus and 56.6 prior, according to preliminary data released by the University of Michigan Survey of Consumers on Friday.

One-year inflation expectations: +3.4% vs. +3.4% in February.

Five-year implied inflation: +3.2% vs. +3.3% prior.

Current economic conditions: 57.8 vs. 56.6 prior.

Consumer expectations: 54.1 vs. 56.6 prior.

Developing... Check back for updates.

data.title: Wheaton hikes dividend after record 2025 revenue, forecasts strong 2026 output growth
data.description:
Wheaton Precious Metals (WPM) +1.4% in early trading Friday after reporting better than expected Q4 adjusted earnings, raising its dividend payout, and forecasting significantly higher production in 2026.

Q4 net earnings soared to US$558.3M from US$88.1M in the year-earlier quarter, as revenues more than doubled to US$864.7M, sparked by higher gold and silver prices and more metals production.

Q4 attributable gold equivalent production rose 8% Y/Y to 205K oz, with gold output up 10% to 130K oz, primarily due to stronger production at Salobo, which achieved a new quarterly record, and Antamina, coupled with the commencement of production at Blackwater.

For the full year, Wheaton (WPM) produced 689,864 gold equivalent oz, up from 635,488 oz in 2024 and exceeding company guidance of 600K-670K oz.

FY 2025 gold production gained 9.2% to 416,171 from 381,248 oz in 2024, silver output rose 6.3% to 22,289 oz from 20,959 oz in 2024, cobalt production nearly doubled to 2,460 oz, while palladium output fell 34% to 10,265 oz from 15,632 in the previous year.

Wheaton (WPM) confirmed the prior guidance for FY 2026 output of 860K-940K gold equivalent oz, to be driven mainly by an additional 70K gold equivalent oz stream from the Antamina site, which will start generating production in April.

data.title: Hormuz blockade: How countries are responding to the global energy crisis
data.description:
As the Iran war rages on, countries around the world are faced with soaring energy prices that could dampen economic growth and boost inflation of necessities.

"The war in the Middle East is creating the largest supply disruption in the history of the global oil market," the International Energy Agency warned this week.

The biggest threat is the closure of the Strait of Hormuz, a narrow waterway through which roughly 20% of the world's oil and LNG supply passes. Most of these shipments go to Asian markets.

To mitigate the disruption, the IEA announced its largest-ever release of oil from the emergency reserves of its 32 members. The IEA will release 400M barrels, more than double the amount it released in 2022 when Russia launched its full-scale invasion of Ukraine.

Crude oil (CL1:COM) — which surged about 30% last week — is on track to post smaller gains for this week even as it crossed $110/bbl, before easing back to the $90 level. The IEA said the ultimate impact on oil and gas markets will depend on the intensity of attacks, damage to energy assets, and, crucially, on how long Hormuz disruptions last.

Here's how countries are responding to the emerging energy crisis:

China

China gets more than a third of its oil and LNG from Arab countries. But China is mostly insulated for now, given its large oil reserves that could last for months. Its government also invested heavily in domestic coal and renewable energy over the years. Even so, Beijing ordered an immediate ban on refined fuel exports and announced the biggest retail fuel price cap increase since 2022. Meanwhile, Iran continues to ship oil to China via the Strait of Hormuz.

Japan

Japan is heavily dependent on the Gulf region for its oil supplies, most of which travel through the Strait of Hormuz. The country has oil reserves equivalent to about 254 days of consumption. Tokyo said it will release about 45 days’ worth of oil supply ‌from its reserves and will reinstate subsidies to effectively cap gasoline prices at around ¥170/ liter. As for LNG, Japan imports only 11% of its supply from Arab countries.

India

More than a third of India’s crude imports pass through the Strait of Hormuz. Since the Iran war broke out, New Delhi shifted 70% of its imports to alternative routes. A bigger crisis is people panic-buying cylinders of liquefied petroleum gas, which is used by most households as cooking fuel. India imports a major portion of its LPG needs, of which 90% comes through the Strait of Hormuz. The government is activating alternate fuel options and boosting domestic LPG production.

South Korea

South Korea imports about 70% of its oil and 20% of its LNG from ⁠the Middle East, and holds sizable oil reserves equivalent to about 207 days’ worth. Seoul announced that it will cap domestic fuel prices, restrict stockpiling of petroleum products and release a record 22.46M barrels from its oil reserves.

Elsewhere in Asia

People are lining up at petrol pumps to stock up on fuel in countries including Vietnam, Pakistan and Bangladesh. Governments introduced measures to conserve energy - Vietnam plans to temporarily remove taxes on fuel imports, Thailand capped some fuel prices and the Philippines is planning a four-day working week for public offices (except fire stations and hospitals). Bangladesh shut universities and imposed fuel rationing for vehicles, while Pakistan plans to close schools for two weeks and introduce a four-day work week for government staff.

Europe

While the European Union gets most of its oil from the U.S. and Norway, the region is exposed to soaring natural gas prices, which push electricity rates higher. A major concern is the U.S. easing sanctions on Russian oil, a move that EU leaders criticized as it would be a boost for Moscow’s war chest. Meanwhile, Germany, France, Spain and Austria said they will release some of their oil reserves. Austria will limit price hikes at fuel pumps to once per day, a move that Germany plans to follow. Hungary and Croatia imposed retail fuel price caps.

Oil exporters

Countries that stand to lose the most from the disruption include Iraq, Qatar, Kuwait, Saudi Arabia and UAE. On the other hand, Russia is viewed as a major beneficiary after the U.S. eased sanctions on its oil. "Sanctioned Russian oil is trading at a premium to the most widely traded international benchmark, which is just mad," Nicholas Mulder, a historian at Cornell University, told Axios. Other oil exporters that are likely to benefit include Norway, Canada and Nigeria.

U.S.

The U.S. said it will release 172M barrels from its Strategic Petroleum Reserve over 120 days. Meanwhile, consumers are feeling the heat at the pump as average gas prices rose 9% compared to last week and 23.5% from a month ago, according to AAA. President Donald Trump signaled that the war won't end any time soon, touting "unlimited ammunition and plenty of time." Iran vowed to continue fighting, and warned that oil could hit $200/bbl. To note, Goldman Sachs raised its odds for a U.S. recession over the next 12 months to 25%, citing the expected stagflationary fallout from the Iran war.
